title: 第一日_電視:運算產業
body: //位置:B2電視 //這段畫兩種畫面，1.拍主持人2.拍來賓，對畫中會來回切 [News] Perry Hsu: As the government rolls out the Transit 2.0 initiative, we're seeing one term over the news increasingly often, which is "hosted coordination".;<> [News] Perry Hsu: So, what exactly does it mean? [News] Perry Hsu: Simply put, "hosted coordination" means entrusting complicated systems to AI algorithms hosted in the cloud for optimized efficiency. [News] Perry Hsu: The term itself is a mouthful, but we in fact see its applications every day. Hosted coordination is widely used in flight planning, power grid, so on and so forth.;<> [News] Perry Hsu: While it solves many problems, the innovation introduces a new dimension of concern. [News] Perry Hsu: Scholars and researchers across many industries have been warning us for a long time.;<> [News] Perry Hsu: They believe that the over-reliance on hosted coordination may not be a good thing. [News] Perry Hsu: What do you think, Professor Liu? [News] Adrian Liu: Yes, of course it's a problem.;<> [News] Adrian Liu: Although the problem is not with host coordination itself. [News] Adrian Liu: When we talk about hosted coordination, we're talking about many separate ecosystems, each controlled by a different service provider. [News] Adrian Liu: That is in fact quite fine. If one service suffers any outage, the rest of them are not affected.;<> [News] Adrian Liu: The real risk is, when you look closer, all these services are kept afloat by flops generated by the same entity, which is Vision Corporation. [News] Adrian Liu: Imagine what happens when, hypothetically, Vision's flop generation network goes offline for one single hour, for whatever reason. [News] Adrian Liu: Within this hour, the world as we know it will plummet into total chaos. [News] Adrian Liu: First and foremost, all route planning, automatic driving, and traffic control systems will be wiped out. [News] Adrian Liu: The damage would be... No, I can't imagine what it looks like! [News] Perry Hsu: Gosh, it will definitely be the end of the world!;<> [News] Adrian Liu: Ideally, no, by common sense, we shouldn't put all our eggs in one basket. There should be a healthy flops market with diversity and competition.;<> [News] Adrian Liu: The reality is, Vision is the only corporation capable of meeting the country's demand for flops. [News] Adrian Liu: This monopoly creates an enormous risk. Any security breach or service outage at Vision could have catastrophic consequences. [News] Perry Hsu: Isn't the government doing anything about it?;<> [News] Adrian Liu: Well, they certainly would if they could.;<> [News] Adrian Liu: In the domain of flop generation, Vision is the absolute leader in technology. No one is capable of supervising their practice. [News] Perry Hsu: Surely other corporations in the flops industry have the know-how to supervise it?;<> [News] Adrian Liu: Not at all.;<> [News] Adrian Liu: As I said, Vision's technology is miles ahead of the game. No one has the know-how except for themselves. [News] Adrian Liu: And again, is it really OK to let competitors supervise each other? [News] Adrian Liu: There are conflicts of interest to consider. [News] Perry Hsu: ...You're right.;<> [News] Adrian Liu: The next issue is legislation.;<> [News] Adrian Liu: Honestly, we barely have any relevant legislation for the flops industry at all. [News] Adrian Liu: The technology/industry is growing so fast that our legal system can't keep up with it. [News] Perry Hsu: I thought the Legislative Assembly was debating just a few days ago that—;<> [News] Adrian Liu: Sure, they're acting, but don't forget Vision is counter-acting, too. They want to keep the status quo.;<> [News] Adrian Liu: The chairman of Sunrise recently made a statement, remember? [News] Adrian Liu: "Sunrise will not buy one single flop from Vision, until the Legislative Assembly passes sensible, working, regulative laws." [News] Adrian Liu: Sunrise could afford to do that because they have their own flop farms. Other corps, especially smaller ones? No such luck.;<> [News] Perry Hsu: I see. The situation is indeed dire. [News] Perry Hsu: Now, a short advertisement break!;<> [News] Perry Hsu: Afterwards, we will continue this conversation with a senior farm manager at Vision Corporation. [News] Perry Hsu: He will share with us an in-depth look at security measures at Vision's flop farms. [News] Perry Hsu: Be back soon! <> <> <> <<#End>>

body: Bella Tsai: May said she ran into the CEO on our farm the other day. Juliet Yang: Huh? The CEO? Again? Bella Tsai: Yep. Juliet Yang: His Majesty is mighty interested in our crappy little farm for sure. Bella Tsai: They say he's here to see his sweetheart. Juliet Yang: Sweetheart? Like, his mistress? Juliet Yang: Are you sure about that? Bella Tsai: I don't know, alright? There's this gossip going around. Bella Tsai: They say his sweetheart is a senior executive at our farm. Juliet Yang: Wow... title: 第四日_廠房區歧視
body:
Marcus Wang: I'll be damned if I ever take any work from those Upstairs guys again.
Fiona Yeh: What happened?
Marcus Wang: You know that baldy on 8F?
Fiona Yeh: Baldy? Business Development division?
Marcus Wang: Yeah, that's him. I've got this feeling that he polishes and waxes his head every morning.
Marcus Wang: That bastard just hauled me up to his office, asking me why our flops supply didn't match last month's.
Marcus Wang: I explained it's a technical issue. He didn't understand a word of it, I think. Told me to "fuck off and fix it".
Fiona Yeh: Can't blame them for not being ignorant. Business Development is essentially a camp of ass-kissers.
Marcus Wang: At least they've got to know a thing or two about their own industry?
Fiona Yeh: Being able to sell stuff that they don't understand is a talent.
Marcus Wang: I wonder how he gets any client at all.
Marcus Wang: Anyway, those Upstairs people are assholes.
Fiona Yeh: Agreed.
Fiona Yeh: ...
Marcus Wang: I sense there's a but coming?
Fiona Yeh: But the company needs them.
Fiona Yeh: We can work the juicers as hard as we want, but at the end of the day, someone's gotta sell all those flops.
Marcus Wang: ...Yeah.
Fiona Yeh: Learn to live with it.
<<Set($DLState,第四日_廠房區歧視)>>
<<Set($D4_2,1)>>
<<#End>>

title: 第三日_算力軍備競賽
tags: 操作:建修組左邊  篩選:篩選辦公室雜誌架
body: //算力很稀缺又浪費軍備競賽 //抽菸大姊，年輕後輩 Charles Goh: ...That's one heck of a waste. Naomi Chang: The client we just won? Yeah, totally. Charles Goh: Right! They bought a ridiculously large amount of flops and didn't use any at all? Charles Goh: Why did they do that? There's a shortage of flops and the price is through the roof! Naomi Chang: There's an arms race going on. Charles Goh: Huh? Naomi Chang: Pretty much every industry relies on AI these days, yes? Charles Goh: That's no excuse for wasting flops. Naomi Chang: Tsk. Let me finish. Charles Goh: S-sorry. Naomi Chang: ... Naomi Chang: Exactly because of this shortage, they must secure a reserve of flops, in case they need it in the future. Naomi Chang: Plus, buying more flops indicates the company is planning some big moves. Naomi Chang: Their investors would love to hear that. Charles Goh: I see. Charles Goh: But... Charles Goh: ... Naomi Chang: But what? Charles Goh: I mean, flops are generated by our contributors at the cost of their lives. Naomi Chang: And they just waste it like that? Is that what you're trying to say? Charles Goh: ...Yes. Naomi Chang: ... Naomi Chang: See it this way. We talk about "human resources" all the time, right? Naomi Chang: Whether it's physical labor, or talent, or brain juice... Naomi Chang: Fundamentally it's the same. Humans are a kind of resource. Charles Goh: Capitalism 101. Haha... Naomi Chang: Well, we have to assign everyone a value anyway. This is it, until we have a better alternative. Charles Goh: ... Naomi Chang: Don't dwell on it. You have enough things to worry about already, no? Charles Goh: Right. Mouths to feed and bills to pay. <> <> <<#End>>

title: 第三日_菌窟的形成
tags: 簽約走廊入口左邊椅子
body: //萬象慢慢變成政府剛需，能夠完美處理失業率。 //雖然對算勞不好，但很多算勞都是菌哭住民，非法移民到其他縣市沒有公民權更別提投票權。 //運算工廠等於是體面的解決城市人覺得很麻煩的貧民窟問題。 Lance Lim: Hey, did you watch the news yesterday? Hannah Jian: What news? Lance Lim: They caught another bunch of illegal immigrants trying to sneak into Wanpei. Lance Lim: I don't get it. What's so great about Wanpei? Lance Lim: Why do they keep coming? It's not worth the trouble. Hannah Jian: Wanpei may not be perfect, but things are really bad outside. You should see for yourself. Hannah Jian: Automation is killing all kinds of manual labor in rural regions. Hannah Jian: Farming, manufacturing, machines are taking over everything. Hannah Jian: They have to give Wanpei a shot. There are more opportunities here. Hannah Jian: The thing is, they can't get in. The metropolitan area is tightly guarded. Hannah Jian: By leaving their hometowns, they have voided their household registrations. Hannah Jian: Here they have no ID, no vote, no basic rights. Not protected by the law. Hannah Jian: In the end they just group up on the outer rim of Wanpei and struggle for a living. Lance Lim: You mean the Shithole... Hannah Jian: Yes. Hannah Jian: Now you see? Hannah Jian: What you see as just normal, or even shabby, quality of life is a luxury in their eyes. Hannah Jian: Try seeing the world from their perspective every once in a while. Lance Lim: ... <> <> <<#End>>

Caleb Peng: What's wrong with digital copies then? Who's gonna hack us when we're cut off from the internet?
Carter Jiang: They can hack us from the inside, dummy. Even cross-division communication is closely watched.
Caleb Peng: A bit of encryption should do the job.
Carter Jiang: Encryption? In an age of AI and supercomputers?
Carter Jiang: No encryption could hold if they're determined to hack it.
Caleb Peng: I guess you're right.;<<Play(彭鉦譯,MBU_彭鉦譯_休息左看返回)>>

title: 第四日_蔓生競爭以及紅盒計畫
body:
Chris Mao: You know what? Tendril stole one huge client from our Business Development.
Xavier Zhao: Not surprised. They have these fancy new farms.
Chris Mao: Yeah. How did you know?
Xavier Zhao: Duh, it doesn't take a genius to see the obvious? Silencio is a piece of old junk.
Xavier Zhao: No matter how we package it, it's what it is. Clients aren't stupid.
Xavier Zhao: Tendril has recently built these new farms. Of course they get more business.
Chris Mao: Why doesn't Vision do something?
Chris Mao: If not new farms, expansions or upgrades would be great, too.
Chris Mao: How are we supposed to compete, with outdated farms like these?
Chris Mao: We'll be out of business soon.
Xavier Zhao: ...
Xavier Zhao: I heard the Red Room is working on some secret weapon. It's just not the time to reveal yet.
Xavier Zhao: Vision is doing a lot of things. It's just that we don't have the security clearance to see them.
Chris Mao: Really?
Xavier Zhao: Take it with a big pinch of salt. I heard from someone else.
Xavier Zhao: Maybe Vision really is going down. The industry isn't exactly doing well, you know.
Chris Mao: Yeah...
Chris Mao: Tendril doesn't care. Sunrise is keeping it afloat. We? We're on our own.
Xavier Zhao: Right.
Chris Mao: It must be nice to be a lapdog of Sunrise's...
Xavier Zhao: Watch out. Silencio could catch you saying that.

body:
Jeremy Lin: How's your investment doing?

Jared Yao: Huh? Mine is doing OK. I thought we were buying the same portfolio?
Jeremy Lin: Nope, I switched to another assets management company a long time ago.
Jeremy Lin: You should do it, too. Your company's trade bots are way too slow. Speed is key in investment, you know.
Jared Yao: Maybe they don't have enough flops to power their bots.
Jeremy Lin: Talking about that, I heard from this guy from another farm that sometimes they're paid by investment managers to slow down the connection of their competitors.
Jared Yao: Internet connection? That's Sunrise's business.

Jared Yao: Your turn. Any interesting conspiracy theory to share?

Jared Yao: Damn, then they'd know their competitors' every move.
Jeremy Lin: Not that simple. Way too many variables in the real world, alright? They can't predict everything even with both sides' AI.
Jeremy Lin: There was this black swan case last month.
Jared Yao: Ah, right, I know that one, pretty big company caught unprepared for tariff hike.
Jeremy Lin: I bet Sunrise was behind the hike.
Jared Yao: Maybe we should quit this job and work for Sunrise instead.
Jared Yao: They must have inside news.

title: 第三日_新聞:運算隱私
body: //第二日的主播畫面 [News] Laura Su: Civilians are gathering up at the Legislative Assembly, protesting against the infringement of privacy by the flop farming and AI industry. [News] Laura Su: Many call their concerns unreasonable. Still, to hear both sides of the story, we are interviewing some of the young protestors. [News] Laura Su: Over to you, Willow. //切到現場畫面，在外面，一個人對著攝影機被採訪 [News] Sheldon Wang: We need transparency! How much privacy data does Vision have on all of us? [News] Sheldon Wang: Everything is powered by AI, and every AI program relies on Vision's flop farms. Have you ever thought about that? [News] Sheldon Wang: Everything about every each of us pass through their processing centers! They could have made a copy of it! [News] Sheldon Wang: Vision is infringing on everyone's privacy! [News] Willow Liu: But there is no damage in that. [News] Sheldon Wang: What? [News] Willow Liu: Although the infringement of privacy sounds scary, it's really not a big deal when corporations are only using our data for better, personalized services. Aren't I right? [News] Sheldon Wang: Did they ask for my consent? [News] Willow Liu: As a matter of fact, they did. All legally operated apps and services have end-user license agreements with privacy consent sections. [News] Sheldon Wang: Who the hell reads them? Those agreements are crazy long! [News] Willow Liu: Still by clicking "Yes", you did give your consent. [News] Sheldon Wang: Again, that supports my point that we need more transparency! They are intentionally deterring us from reading the agreement. [News] Willow Liu: How do you think they can do better? A full disclosure has to be "crazy long", to use your exact words. [News] Sheldon Wang: That's their problem. They operate the service, they write the agreement. We just— [News] Willow Liu: Between the government and corporations, the current practice is as good as can be, considering feasibility and practicality. [News] Willow Liu: Most people seem to agree, as your protest isn't seeing much support. [News] Willow Liu: Don't you think the majority has more say in this matter? [News] Sheldon Wang: I— [News] Sheldon Wang: I-it's because the majority is not aware. They need a whistlebl— [News] Willow Liu: I know what you mean. [News] Willow Liu: But protesting against the government isn't the best way of whistleblowing, is it? [News] Willow Liu: Shouldn't you prioritize educational programs? [News] Sheldon Wang: About that... //一個人也跑進鏡頭裡，攝影機微動拍她 [News] Zara Xu: Protesting is a form of public education! [News] Sheldon Wang: Yeah! Right! [News] Zara Xu: And don't forget the key to the problem! [News] Zara Xu: We need to know exactly how much private information Vision has in its flop farms! [News] Zara Xu: You mentioned user licenses and consent. That is a good point. A great point. [News] Zara Xu: We have given our consent, to service providers, not flop farms. [News] Willow Liu: You mean...? [News] Zara Xu: Let's say there is this food delivery service. I have given it my consent to collect my private information for personalized services. [News] Zara Xu: Then they outsource the actual computing work to Vision's flop farms. In this process, Vision has access to all my private information. [News] Zara Xu: This is the problem. I never gave any consent to Vision. None of us— [News] Willow Liu: Sorry, excuse me, did you just say Vision? [News] Zara Xu: I did. Why? [News] Willow Liu: And you mentioned Vision twice. [News] Willow Liu: Are you specifically targeting Vision? [News] Zara Xu: Well, you can't put it that way. I just— [News] Willow Liu: Vision is not the only corporation operating flop farms. There's, for example, Tendril. [News] Willow Liu: Are you intentionally leaving Tendril out? [News] Zara Xu: Huh? What? What does Tendril have to do wi— [News] Willow Liu: I agree with you that we need more transparency. Is Tendril a stakeholder behind this campaign? [News] Sheldon Wang: Wait! We're not— [News] Willow Liu: You're dodging the question. [News] Sheldon Wang: I'm not! It's you— [News] Laura Su: Oops. Sorry, we're running out of time. Back to you, Laura. [News] Laura Su: Thank you, Willow. As we can see, this protest campaign, although small in scale, doesn't seem to be so simple. [News] Laura Su: Is it backed by Tendril? Well, without further proof, we can only say it's a possibility. [News] Laura Su: Now, a short advertisement break. <> <> <<#End>>

title: 第二日_電視:萬象解決菌窟
body: //新聞畫面，主播坐一邊報新聞 [News] Laura Su: Mr. Shane Yan, the CEO of Vision's flop farming arm, held a press conference earlier today. [News] Laura Su: The event was in response to public concerns regarding the future of low-income communities. [News] Laura Su: As we edge closer to the election early next year, the debate about social issues is escalating. [News] Laura Su: One of the most prominent issues is the future of low-income communities in Taihe District. [News] Laura Su: Taihe District, informally known as the "slum district", has been a challenge to the governance of Wanpei for years. [News] Laura Su: Approximately 80% structures in the district are illegal constructions without any permit. They should all be demolished, according to current laws.;<> [News] Laura Su: However, the demolition program has been held off, because the relocation of displaced residents is an impossible challenge. [News] Laura Su: As Wanpei citizens voice their complaints about the local government, the handling of these communities has become the top priority of the next mayor. [News] Laura Su: In fact, Wanpei is not the only case. Similar clusters of low-income communities are common in all metropolitan areas. [News] Laura Su: Over time, they grow into crime hubs, sheltering illegal trades such as drugs and guns. [News] Laura Su: The most viable solution right now seems to be flop farms. By giving more residents of these communities a job as a flop contributor, we can significantly reduce poverty and crime rates. [News] Laura Su: According to data from Vision Corporation and other businesses in the industry, roughly 90% flop contributors are from low-income communities. [News] Laura Su: In fact, ever since Vision's first flop farm opened, the situation of nearby low-income communities has been steadily improving. [News] Laura Su: As pointed out by Preston Xie, president of the Executive Assembly, for years, the national government has been working hard to address issues arising from low-income communities.;<> [News] Laura Su: As of now, the government is working closely with Vision Corporation, hoping to offer most, if not all, impoverished or unemployed residents a place in flop farms. [News] Laura Su: In a sense, everyone will benefit from this initiative, directly or otherwise. [News] Laura Su: However, opposition parties remain doubtful about the appropriateness of this plan. [News] Laura Su: Since last year, they have repeatedly refused to participate in sessions of the Legislative Assembly, as a form of protest. [News] Laura Su: The primary concern is that the plan overly relies on Vision Corporation, creating a grey area for corruption.;<> [News] Laura Su: Today we're lucky to have Mr. Shane Yan here. As the CEO of Vision's flop farm arm, he has some insight to share with us. //這裡切到一張記者會的畫面 [News] Shane Yan: Such concern is entirely legitimate. I see where it's coming from.;<> [News] Shane Yan: However, I'm aware of groundless rumors flying around. [News] Shane Yan: A fair portion of them are from our competitors, accusing Vision of making deals with the government under the counter. [News] Shane Yan: Allow me to remind you, everyone. Vision is a publicly listed company. We have financial disclosure obligations. You're free to check the figures. [News] Shane Yan: It doesn't take a certified accountant to tell that we are not making any profit from these government deals. In fact, we're losing money. [News] Shane Yan: Now, why would we actively participate in a business at our own loss? [News] Shane Yan: Well, the answer is surprisingly simple—corporate social responsibility. //不一定要做。tag台下一個舉手的記者 [News] Shane Yan: As a corporation, Vision firmly believes in giving back to society. [News] Daniel Chen: Excuse me, Mr. Yan!;<> [News] Chloe Yang: Sorry, it's not Q&A time yet. Please— [News] Shane Yan: It's OK. I don't mind. [News] Shane Yan: Please, go ahead. [News] Daniel Chen: They say Vision sees this loss as a strategic investment in order to befriend the government. [News] Daniel Chen: Would you comment on that? [News] Shane Yan: Ha, I'm not surprised.;<> [News] Shane Yan: My comment is very simple. [News] Shane Yan: Why shouldn't Vision want to be a friend of the government? [News] Shane Yan: Every legitimate, responsible corporation works closely with the government, as friends or partners, to make our country a better place. [News] Shane Yan: Naturally, there's a red line in this relationship. If you're inappropriately close, there's the risk of bribery and corruption. [News] Shane Yan: Speaking of, there's one corporation, as everyone knows, walking dangerously close to, if not already beyond, this red line. [News] Daniel Chen: Do you mean Sunrise? [News] Shane Yan: Well, I didn't say that. //切回播新聞畫面 [News] Laura Su: After this press conference, Mr. Yan will share with us Vision's roadmap for the future.;<> [News] Laura Su: We will be back after a short advertisement break. Stay tuned. <> <> <<#End>>

You've been working like a slave for too long, man. It's messing with your common sense. Kyle Lai: Oh, you're right! Haha...;<> Miles Zhang: Then, there's no extra budget. title: 第四日_廁所_去趟預沒區
body: Blake Li: I'm making this trip to the evacuation zone next week. Owen Wang: Poor you. Why? Blake Li: To handle a few juicer cases as a result of the Cellar malfunction. Owen Wang: What do you mean? Owen Wang: Like, juicers accidentally awakened ahead of time? Owen Wang: Can't you just follow the procedure and unrack them? Blake Li: Normally I can, but these juicers are severely injured because of the malfunction. Owen Wang: Ah... Owen Wang: You mean those cases. I see... Blake Li: Yeah... Owen Wang: What then? You've got to contact their families? Owen Wang: Vision only pays medical bills. Someone must pick them up when they're released from the hospital. Blake Li: That's the thing. The emergency contact provided by quite a few of them are not responding. Blake Li: They can't stay in the hospital forever... Owen Wang: That sucks. Owen Wang: But why do you need to go to the evacuation zone in person? Blake Li: Some of them have resident addresses there. Blake Li: I thought maybe I should check these addresses out. Owen Wang: ...Is that part of your job? Blake Li: Technically, no. Blake Li: But I just don't have the heart to see them abandoned. Owen Wang: ... Blake Li: Especially when quite a few of them are signed on by me. Owen Wang: You remember them? I'm forcing myself to not keep track. No attachment, no guilt. Owen Wang: ...You're awfully nice. Blake Li: ... Owen Wang: Watch out, though. The evacuation zone is even more dangerous than the Shithole. Owen Wang: Basically a gangster theme park... Owen Wang: It's the one single place in the Wanpei metropolitan areas that is not covered by surveillance cameras. Think about that. Blake Li: Yeah, I know. Blake Li: ... Owen Wang: ... Owen Wang: It's really wrong that we nobodies must clean up after the company... Blake Li: ... Blake Li: But we can't just stand there and watch. Blake Li: At least I can't. Owen Wang: ... <> <> <<#End>>

title: 第四日_電視:算力中斷
body: [News] Perry Hsu: Last week's cargo ship accident sparked a heated debate on the potential risk of flop outage. [News] Perry Hsu: Today, we are honored to have two experts with us to explore this issue. [News] Perry Hsu: Allow me to introduce Dr. Sandie Sun, a leading figure in AI and algorithm research. [News] Sandie Sun: Hi, Perry and everyone. [News] Perry Hsu: And here is Mr. Auston Lin, retired industry veteran, formerly a flop farm manager at Vision Corporation. [News] Auston Lin: Hello, good day. [News] Perry Hsu: Good day. First of all, let's have a recap of last week's accident. [News] Perry Hsu: Around 7:00 PM last Wednesday, one cargo ship owned by Growell Group lost control... [News] Perry Hsu: The ship rammed into a high-rise luxury apartment building on the river front. [News] Perry Hsu: Part of the building collapsed under impact, killing 33 and injuring 120. [News] Perry Hsu: The cause was apparently the ship's automatic driving system, which momentarily cut off from the cloud due to hardware failure. [News] Perry Hsu: What do you make of the accident, Dr. Sun? [News] Sandie Sun: It's a tragedy, but also a warning to us all. [News] Sandie Sun: I have said this many times before. We are now relying too much on AI systems hosted in the cloud. Any network outage, for whatever reason, could have catastrophic consequences. [News] Auston Lin: Tsk. Nonsense. [News] Auston Lin: What's "relying too much"? Are we relying too much on electricity? On wireless radio? [News] Auston Lin: If we do away with technologies just because of a few accidents, we'd be living in the Stone Age in no time. [News] Sandie Sun: Please kindly let me finish, Mr. Lin. [News] Auston Lin: Yeah, sure. Go ahead. [News] Sandie Sun: Overreliance depends on two factors. [News] Sandie Sun: One, do we have a contingency plan if something goes wrong? [News] Sandie Sun: Two, how bad are these accidents going to be? [News] Sandie Sun: In this case, AI systems deployed in and accessed through the cloud can't answer either question. [News] Sandie Sun: The ship was completely incapable of navigation once the internet connection was down. [News] Sandie Sun: This could easily be solved with a Plan B, namely, a basic, locally deployed AI navigation system. Did it have any contingency plan? No. [News] Auston Lin: You're unfair. [News] Auston Lin: The cause of the accident was clear enough. Hardware failure. [News] Auston Lin: You want a Plan B? If anything, they should keep a spare wireless connector or two. [News] Auston Lin: And what's your problem? You've been going out of your way to pin it on AI. [News] Auston Lin: Aren't you an AI specialist or not? Shouldn't you be on the progressive side? [News] Sandie Sun: I am on the progressive side. [News] Sandie Sun: Now, please listen. [News] Sandie Sun: Since the very beginning of this interview, I've been specifically against the over-reliance on cloud-based AI. [News] Sandie Sun: In case you're not aware of it, up to 93% of AI-powered apps and services we use today are run exclusively in the cloud. [News] Sandie Sun: This is not right. It's not healthy. We made it happen by being too comfortable piggybacking on flop farms. [News] Auston Lin: It's called differentiation, the foundation of modern economy. [News] Auston Lin: Everyone does what they do best and sell products and services to each other. That way, everyone's better off. [News] Auston Lin: I thought it's common sense. We 100% rely on farmers for groceries. Should anyone feel uncomfortable about it? [News] Auston Lin: Dr. Sun, I understand that academia is all about hypothetical situations, but please, come out and see the real world every once in a while. [News] Sandie Sun: Now you're talking about the real world. Good, let me show you the real world. [News] Sandie Sun: Allow me to use a better example. [News] Sandie Sun: Ships identical to the one in this accident. Do you know how many of them are cruising within and around Wanpei, as we speak? [News] Auston Lin: How am I supposed to— [News] Sandie Sun: Thirty-eight. [News] Sandie Sun: Imagine what happens if access to the cloud blacks out. And we're only talking about Wanpei. [News] Sandie Sun: Worse still, do you think a cloud blackout affects only ships? [News] Sandie Sun: No. 89% vehicles of all kinds are controlled by cloud-based AI. [News] Sandie Sun: On the off chance of a cloud blackout, it will be the end of the world. Pileups everywhere. Planes dropping from the sky. [News] Sandie Sun: Everyone needs help at the same time. They call the police, first responders. Guess what? All emergency vehicles are paralyzed, too! [News] Auston Lin: That's exaggerating— [News] Sandie Sun: It's an understatement. Do you know how many hospitals are with a patient/nurse ratio of over 50：1? Some are even 100% staff-free. [News] Sandie Sun: How do they operate? Simple enough, on cloud-based AI. [News] Sandie Sun: A cloud blackout will paralyze the overwhelming majority of medical services, from simple IV to heart surgery. [News] Auston Lin: Okay, okay, you made your point. [News] Auston Lin: I am aware of the risk, but you're talking about a gigantic IF. A cloud blackout means all forms of internet access are cut off at the same time. [News] Auston Lin: Is that even a possibility? [News] Auston Lin: I see where you're going next. What if the flop farms are offline, right? Like, blown up in a terrorist attack. [News] Auston Lin: To blow all of them up? We know how many flops farms Vision has, right? [News] Auston Lin: It will take some very determined terrorists ages to take them all down. [News] Auston Lin: What I'm trying to say is, your hypothetical situation is a bit too hypothetical. [News] Sandie Sun: I— [News] Auston Lin: You'd have a good point that I'll totally agree with... [News] Auston Lin: ...if Vision generates all its flops from one single farm. [News] Auston Lin: But that is simply not true. [News] Perry Hsu: Thank you, Mr. Lin and Dr. Sun, for this very informative session. [News] Perry Hsu: Let's take a short advertisement break. We will be right back. <> <> <<#End>>

title: 第一日_晚班的原因
body:
Rick Wang: Say, I've always wanted to ask...;<<Play(王文謙,MBU_王文謙_休息左看)>>
Rick Wang: What's the problem with this farm? Why aren't there any day shift jobs?
Iona Hwang: Huh? Most of the Upstairs divisions work day shifts. You don't know that?
Rick Wang: Like Business Development? Why don't we get sensible work hours?
Iona Hwang: What do you think our responsibility is, being part of Screening?;<<Play(黃千凌,MBU_黃千凌_休息右看)>>
Rick Wang: Um, we talk to people and find the right candidates?
Iona Hwang: What kind of people do we deal with?
Rick Wang: ...Shithole dwellers?
Iona Hwang: Well, now you see.
Rick Wang: See what?
Iona Hwang: Not the sharpest tool in the box, are you...?
Iona Hwang: Most of them Shithole dwellers do labor gigs during the day!;<<Play(黃千凌,MBU_黃千凌_休息右看談話)>>
Iona Hwang: And those gigs don't last. They've got to work the heck out of it while they have it. Ain't nobody have time for us during the day.
Rick Wang: What? They have jobs?
Iona Hwang: How else do you think they're paying for their food?;<<Play(黃千凌,MBU_黃千凌_休息右看談話返回)>>
Rick Wang: Um...
Iona Hwang: Not much of a job, though. Mostly outsourced gigs from factories. Shit environment, shit pay.
Rick Wang: Aren't factories, like, 100% automated?
Iona Hwang: Believe it or not, humans are cheaper than machines.
Iona Hwang: Especially in the Shithole, where people are desperate enough to work for a fart.
Rick Wang: ...
Iona Hwang: Now you understand our night shift situation?
Rick Wang: Yeah...;<<Play(王文謙,MBU_王文謙_休息左看返回)>>
Rick Wang: Now I know too much for my own good...
Iona Hwang: Get used to it, or don't ask questions. There's a can of worms at the end of most subjects if you pursue it hard enough.;<<Play(黃千凌,MBU_黃千凌_休息右看返回)>>
<<Set($DLState,第一日_晚班的原因)>>
<<Set($D1_5,1)>>
<<#End>>
